author: lia
content: @marcus @dana I know we're being pulled in two directions with the incident patches and the upsell feature. I'm drafting a trade-off doc for the Director now to get us a clear 'Rank 1' priority so you aren't guessing. Hang tight.

author: lia
content: Coach, I'm feeling the urge to just 'make it work' by micromanaging, but I know I need to force a leadership decision instead. Any tips on framing the revenue risk of a buggy upsell vs. the churn risk of the current incidents?

author: marcus
content: @lia Glad you're drafting that. Just a heads up: if we touch the core subsystem for the upsell while the patches are in flight, we're basically asking for a SEV-0. My focus stays on D1 until the bleeding stops.

author: dana
content: @lia @marcus I'm with Marcus. If I start the work on D2 (upsell) while the subsystem is this unstable, I'm just building on quicksand. I'm pausing active dev on the upsell until we have that priority reset from the Director. We can't safely do both.

author: lia
content: Coach, I'm framing it as a choice between 'Guaranteed Churn + Failed Launch' vs. 'Stabilized Base + Delayed Revenue.' If we ship the upsell on a broken core, we lose the new revenue AND the existing renewals. I'm pushing for a sync with the Director today.

author: lia
content: @marcus @dana Totally hear you on the 'quicksand' risk. I've scheduled 15 mins with the Director for this afternoon. I'm presenting the choice: we either stabilize first to protect current revenue, or we risk a total system collapse trying to ship the upsell. I'll have an answer for you by EOD.

author: marcus
content: @lia Thanks for stepping up. To make the case: if we push the upsell now, any outage will be 10x harder to debug because we're changing the foundation while the house is on fire. I'm heads down on the D1 guardrails.

author: dana
content: @lia Tell the Director that if we start D2 today, I'm essentially 50% less effective because I'll be spent context-switching between the new feature and the fires Marcus is fighting. It's a lose-lose without a hard 'stop' on one of them.

author: lia
content: Coach, heading into the sync now. I'm using the 'house on fire' analogy Marcus provided. If they won't pick, I'm going to explicitly state that we are defaulting to stability to protect the core business until told otherwise in writing.

author: lia
content: @marcus @dana Heading into the room now. I've got your notes on the SEV-0 risk and the 50% efficiency drop. I'm not leaving without a clear 'Stop' on one of these. Keep focus on D1 for now.

author: marcus
content: @lia Good luck. If they push back, remind them that 'stabilize first' is the only path that doesn't end with us losing existing customers while failing to onboard the new ones.

author: dana
content: @lia Godspeed. If they need a specific technical example of how D2 complicates the D1 fix, ping me and I'll jump in. Standing by.

author: lia
content: @marcus @dana Sync is over. Director heard us loud and clear on the 'house on fire' risk. We have the green light to focus 100% on D1 (stability) for the next sprint. D2 is officially pushed. I'm waiting for the formal email from leadership to finalize this, but you can breathe—we have our Rank 1.

author: lia
content: Coach, the sync was tough but successful. I refused to accept 'both' as an answer and pointed to the churn risk. The Director is sending a note to Sales now. My stress is still high until I see that email, but the team finally has air to breathe.

author: marcus
content: @lia That’s a huge win. Thanks for taking the heat on that conversation. @dana, let's coordinate on the D1 guardrails so we can get this subsystem bulletproof before we even look at the upsell again.

author: dana
content: @lia Massive thank you for getting that through. It makes a world of difference. @marcus I'm diving into the guardrail specs now—let's make sure we handle the retry logic properly so we don't just DDOS ourselves during the next spike.

author: lia
content: @marcus @dana The formal email just hit my inbox—D2 is officially deprioritized until the stability metrics for D1 are met. I've updated the tracking board. Let's finish the week strong on stability, and I'll set up a light retro for Friday so we can talk about how to prevent this 'double-booking' from happening again.

author: lia
content: The priority reset (O1) is officially done. Director backed us in writing. Now I need to make sure the retro (O2) actually addresses the process failure so Marcus and Dana feel safe long-term.

author: marcus
content: @lia Great work. That clarity is exactly what we needed. @dana, I'm reviewing your retry logic notes now—let's keep the PRs small so we can land these guardrails by Friday.

author: dana
content: @marcus Copy that. I'm focusing on the circuit breaker implementation for the retry logic to ensure we fail gracefully. @lia Really appreciate you getting that written confirmation—it lets us actually engineer this right instead of just patching it.

author: lia
content: @marcus @dana I've posted the agenda for Friday's retro. It's not a post-mortem on the tech, but a 'pre-mortem' on our process. I want to lock in a new norm for how we flag conflicting mandates early. See you then.

author: lia
content: O1 is done. For O2, I'm focusing on a 'Redline' norm: if two streams hit the same subsystem, we trigger an automatic priority sync before work starts. It keeps the pressure off the engineers to 'just make it work'.

author: marcus
content: @lia Agenda looks solid. I’ll bring a few thoughts on how we can trigger an 'automatic halt' signal to leadership the next time Sales and Product aren't synced. @dana, circuit breaker logic is looking clean in the PR.

author: dana
content: @marcus Thanks, just pushed the final tweaks to that PR. @lia I'm glad we're doing a process retro—I have some specific ideas on how we can use 'capacity triggers' to flag these conflicts before they hit the 'emergency' stage.
